item.title: Investigating the division of scientific labor using the Contributor Roles Taxonomy (CRediT)
item.description: Paper Vincent Larivière, David Pontille, Cassidy R. Sugimoto; Investigating the division of scientific labor using the Contributor Roles Taxonomy (CRediT).

item.title: The network structure of scientific revolutions
item.description: Paper Philosophy of science Karl Popper: the development of scientific ideas as a sequence in which previous theories are falsified.

item.title: Network neuroscience
item.description: Paper Bassett, D., Sporns, O. Network neuroscience. Nat Neurosci 20, 353–364 (2017).

item.title: Element-centric clustering comparison unifies overlaps and hierarchy
item.description: Paper Gates, A.J., Wood, I.B., Hetrick, W.P. et al. Element-centric clustering comparison unifies overlaps and hierarchy.

item.title: Hunters, busybodies, and the knowledge network building associated with curiosity
item.description: Paper Lydon-Staley, D. M., Zhou, D., Blevins, A. S., Zurn, P.

item.title: Large teams develop and small teams disrupt science and technology
item.description:
Paper

Wu, L., Wang, D. & Evans, J.A. Large teams develop and small teams disrupt science and technology. Nature 566, 378–382 (2019). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-019-0941-9

Summary

Small teams tend to disrupt science and technology with new ideas and opportunities while large teams tend to develop existing one.

  1. One of the most universal trends in science and technology is that team size the growth of the team size.
  2. New Question: Whether and how the character of the scienc and technology produced by large teams differs from that of small teams
  3. Work from larger teams builds on more-recent and popular developments, and attention to their work comes immediately.
  4. Contributions by smaller teams search more deeply into the past, are viewed as disruptive to science and technology and succeed further into the future—if at all.

item.title: Topic diversity, A discipline scheme-free diversity measurement for journals
item.description:
Paper

ToreOpsahl (2013). Triadic closure in two-mode networks: Redefining the global and local clustering coefficients. Social Networks, 35(2), 159-167. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socnet.2011.07.001

Summary

It’s about the how to quantify journals' diversity by utilizing the abstracts of scientfic pulciations in journals.

by making graph of words and using clustering dtection technique

  1. Word extraction

so they tokenize the abstracts and get some meaningful words from the abstracts. they said they used TextRank.. I don’t know much about it so I have to study further.

  1. Network Construction they gathered synonym or cognate words into one set. and this same meaning word set becomes a one node of the network

then the edge connection weighted as their cooccurrence frequency.

  1. Topic detection For topic detection step, they exploit Louvain method

they recursively exploit Louvain algorithm so that they can even get subfield of the first cluster.

So for validation tehy also repeat this steps several times and check how degree within the community is stable.

  1. Diversity Calculation

In this step, they exploit diversity calulator developed by Leinseter.

Using their caclulcated function where input variables are words and out variables are the topics and the diversity calculator, they get diversity of

item.title: A neuromarker of sustained attention from whole-brain functional connectivity
item.description:
Paper

Rosenberg, M., Finn, E., Scheinost, D. et al. A neuromarker of sustained attention from whole-brain functional connectivity. Nat Neurosci 19, 165–171 (2016). https://doi.org/10.1038/nn.4179

Summary

  1. The authors identified that functional brain netoworks whose strength during a sustained attention task predicted individual differences in attentional performance.

  2. Furthermorethe model suggested in the paper also be able to predict a clinical measure of attention (ADHD) from resting state connectivity in an independent sample of children and adolsecents.

item.title: Using connectome-based predictive modeling to predict individual behavior from brain connectivity
item.description:
Paper

Shen, X., Finn, E. S., Scheinost, D., Rosenberg, M. D., Chun, M. M., Papademetris, X., & Constable, R. T. (2017). Using connectome-based predictive modeling to predict individual behavior from brain connectivity. Nature protocols, 12(3), 506–518. https://doi.org/10.1038/nprot.2016.178

Summary

A new model to predict behavioral data with the brain connectivity.

[Steps]

  1. Feature selection

  2. Feature Summarization

  3. Model Building

  4. Assessment of Prediction Significance.

Interesting part : It only uses linear model.

item.title: Hierarchical organization of cortical and thalamic connectivity
